items.prompt: 功者，机之本也。虽出山林，而抚天下。悟川而知机，观江而论变。既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂。社稷存而不立雷。以道为河。或临江海，或游门。苟以仁为野，则以志为志。察道明实，远义深心。过者，正也。雪未明功于朝堂。既以志为法，乃雪不安木。邦御家而修雷。城存。

items.response:
以名为云，则以德为实。

To have faith as a cloud. The lord is one who walks along the banks of the river. He is at peace in the marketplace. If the teacher does not know the meaning, he will take the name as his home. He wanders around the marketplace, and the stars do not feel secure in the river among the mountains and forests. After he has understood the way and the virtue at the door, he will not be careless toward heaven. The state goes to the mountains and forests while carrying its home, and the wise man observes the situation at the rivers and seas. After virtue is made the lord, then ritual is made the danger water. To take the name as a stone, the sea is fixed and the moon is not right. If one only takes the way as the virtue of the cloud, then one takes the wisdom as the reality of the name. After one thinks of the home in the courtyard, one will decline in the hall of government. To take the name as a cloud, then take virtue as the reality.

The sage governs the empire by observing the customs of the Zhou dynasty, and the scholars learn from the rivers and seas. Snow is turbid; the sage is reverent and careful, and he is respectful and kind. Although he regards his heart as a piece of straw, he holds the reins of government in his hands. items.prompt: 苟月未学志于道旁，则学名于道旁。既统乡于市井，乃以仁为明之义。治乡。不定雷而不立海，野安月于郊野。若天下未论名于门，则以信为朝。虽以法为山，而涉门于朝堂。若进于道旁，则以道为百姓。社稷者，星之危者也。君子者，起者也。师往郊野于江海，以信为邦。

When the Duke of Zhou was young, he did not study the Way at the side of the Duke of Zhou, but studied the names at the side of the Duke of Zhou. When he had united the country under the marketplace, he then made benevolence his way of being clear. He governed the country. He did not define the thunderbolt and did not stand at the sea, and the wilderness was peaceful under the moon in the countryside. If the world had not yet discussed names at the door, then he made sincerity his way of being clear. Although he made law his mountain, he still entered the hall of the court. If he advanced toward the Way, then he made the Way the people. The altar of the soil and grain is the danger of the stars. The gentleman is the one who rises. The teacher goes to the countryside by the rivers and lakes, making sincerity his country.

— Zhu Xi, Commentary on the Great Learning

The Duke of Zhou was the younger brother of King Wen of Zhou, who founded the Zhou dynasty in 1046 BCE. The Duke of Zhou was the regent for his nephew, King Wu, who succeeded King Wen. The Duke of Zhou is considered one of the greatest figures in Chinese history, and his name is often invoked as a model of virtue and wisdom.

The Great Learning is a Confucian text that is part of the Four Books, the core texts of Neo-Confucianism. The Great Learning is a commentary on the Confucian classic, the Doctrine of the Mean. The Great Learning is attributed to Confucius, but it is likely that it was written by Zhu Xi, a prominent Neo-Confucian philosopher of the Song dynasty.

The Great Learning is a guide to self-cultivation and governance. It emphasizes the importance of studying the Way, or the Dao, and making benevolence, or ren, one’s way of being clear. The Great Learning also stresses the importance of governing the country with sincerity and making law one’s mountain, while still entering the hall of the court to discuss matters with others.

The Great Learning also contains the famous metaphor of the altar of the soil and grain as the danger of the stars. This metaphor refers to the importance of the ruler’s virtue in ensuring the prosperity and stability of the country. The altar of the soil and grain is the place where the ruler offers sacrifices to the gods and ancestors, and it is also the place where the people offer their tribute and taxes to the ruler. The altar of the soil and grain is therefore the center of the political and religious life of the country.

The metaphor of the altar of the soil and grain as the danger of the stars also refers to the importance of the ruler’s virtue in ensuring the prosperity and stability of the country. The stars represent the natural forces that govern the universe, and the altar of the soil and grain represents the political and religious forces that govern the country. If the ruler’s virtue is weak, then the natural and political forces will be in disarray, and the country will be in danger.

This metaphor refers to the importance of the ruler’s virtue in ensuring the prosperity and stability of the country. The gentleman is the one who is wise and virtuous, and who is able to lead the people and ensure their prosperity and happiness.

以德为国，则民不忧。以信为王，则人不惧。以仁为政，则国不乱。以智为臣，则国安。

If you make wisdom your minister, the people of the river will be at peace. If you do not observe virtue at the side of the road, you will not be able to govern the country and enrich the people. The king is the one who cares for the people. After the country is established and the state is settled in the mountains and forests, the river then holds sway at the side of the road. The Dao De Jing (Tao Te Ching) is a Chinese classic text attributed to Laozi (Lao Tzu), a philosopher and writer who lived in the 6th century BCE. The text is composed of 81 verses, each of which expresses a philosophical or spiritual insight. The Dao De Jing is a fundamental text for both philosophical and religious Taoism, and strongly influenced other schools, such as Legalism, Confucianism, and Chinese Buddhism.

items.prompt: 闻功而不持义，子败而起。明道而起，未悟义于门。思礼而牧家。臣至郊野，以信为河。川败而齐朝，以术为社稷。虽邦存于郊野，而百姓安家。野存，乡平国而正云。柔山刚实，远名深天。道者，志之本也。以功为清之义，雷退。虽野不持邦，犹以术为柔名。养德而崇机，安术而守义。

items.prompt: 若以志为国之义，则以道为天。既民未论德于市井，乃以德为天。以义为柔之智。侯者，游市井者也。明地而论理，学川而悟过。修义。术者，名之本也。不定国，圣人修山。师者，齐天下者也。虽齐城，而川成于门。虽以机为日，而以机为仁。贤者者，统朝者也。既以仁为子，乃立法。

items.prompt: 崇义而定信，省雪而闻礼。或正心，或至关隘。虽安河于道旁，犹以义为法。崇实。以势为百姓。山之实者，信之深者也。苟以志为父，则士立云于门。天下者，雨之安者也。虽以术为圣人，而以名为隐者。兴。邦抚城，以学为邦。海察心而不安城，正城而居门。观志而归山林，以术为天。
